original_hypothesis.title: `VoteWeighting` Checkpoint Stagnation if Missed for Over 53 Weeks
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: If a nominee or the global sum is not checkpointed for more than 53 weeks, the state advancement loop completes without updating the timestamp, causing an infinite loop of stagnation where the state can never catch up to the present.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: In `VoteWeighting._getSum()` and `_getWeight()`, a loop advances the state week-by-week up to `MAX_NUM_WEEKS` (53). The timestamp `timeSum` is only updated if `t > block.timestamp`. If the time elapsed since the last checkpoint is > 53 weeks, the loop finishes 53 iterations, `t` remains `<= block.timestamp`, and `timeSum` is never updated. Subsequent calls will start from the exact same old `timeSum`, recalculate the same 53 weeks, and again fail to advance, permanently bricking the state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop bound prevents state progression: `if (t > block.timestamp) { timeSum = t; }` is unreachable if elapsed time > `MAX_NUM_WEEKS`.

preconditions: No checkpoint is called for a specific nominee or the global sum for > 53 weeks.

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `timeSum` and `timeWeight` are not updated if the loop hits its maximum iterations before reaching the current time.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No unconditional update of `timeSum = t` at the end of the loop.

analysis_questions: Can an attacker intentionally brick a specific nominee by creating it and ignoring it for 54 weeks?
Does bricking a single nominee affect the global `timeSum`?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract, wait 54 weeks, call `checkpoint()`, and observe that `timeSum` does not advance.

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker adds a dummy nominee and does not vote for it. After 54 weeks, the attacker calls `checkpointNominee`. The nominee's `timeWeight` fails to advance. While this only bricks the dummy nominee, if the entire protocol is paused or inactive for 54 weeks, the global `timeSum` becomes permanently bricked, halting all voting and reward distribution.
prerequisites: 54 weeks of inactivity for a specific state variable

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS of gauge weight calculations.

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for extreme time advancement (> 1 year) between checkpoints.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Check for `timeSum` values significantly lagging behind `block.timestamp`.

validation_result.impact_summary: Permanent DoS of gauge weight calculations for any nominee (or the global sum) inactive for > 53 weeks, resulting in zero staking incentives.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `VoteWeighting` contract uses a catch-up loop in `_getSum()` and `_getWeight()` to advance the state of the global weight sum and individual nominee weights week-by-week. To prevent block gas limit exhaustion, the loop is bounded by `MAX_NUM_WEEKS` (53). 

However, the state variables tracking the last updated time (`timeSum` and `timeWeight[nomineeHash]`) are only updated inside the loop under the condition `if (t > block.timestamp)`. If a nominee or the global sum has not been checkpointed for more than 53 weeks, the loop will execute its maximum 53 iterations. At the end of the 53rd iteration, the advanced time `t` will still be less than or equal to `block.timestamp`. As a result, the condition `t > block.timestamp` evaluates to false, and the state variable (`timeSum` or `timeWeight`) is NEVER updated.

Because the state variable is not updated, subsequent calls to `_getSum()` or `_getWeight()` will start from the exact same stale timestamp, recalculate the exact same 53 weeks, and again fail to update the timestamp. This creates an infinite loop of stagnation where the state can never catch up to the present. 

When the `Dispenser` attempts to calculate staking incentives for a bricked nominee, it queries `nomineeRelativeWeight` for the current epoch's end time. Since the catch-up loop never reached the current epoch, the recorded bias for that timestamp is 0. Consequently, the nominee receives 0 staking incentives permanently, rendering it dead.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerability is clearly visible in the source code of `VoteWeighting.sol`. The conditional update of the state variables `timeSum` and `timeWeight` at the end of the loop iterations fails to account for pagination when the time gap exceeds the loop bound.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: If the time elapsed since the last checkpoint is > 53 weeks, the loop completes 53 iterations. At the end, `t` is still `<= block.timestamp`, so `timeWeight[nomineeHash] = t` is skipped. The state never advances.
Identical logic flaw for the global `timeSum`. If the protocol is inactive for > 53 weeks, the global sum permanently bricks, halting all reward distributions.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When `checkpointNominee` is called after 54 weeks of inactivity, `_getWeight` initializes `t = timeWeight[nomineeHash]`. The loop runs for `MAX_NUM_WEEKS` (53) iterations, incrementing `t` by `WEEK` each time. After 53 iterations, `t` is `timeWeight + 53 * WEEK`. Because `t <= block.timestamp`, the condition `if (t > block.timestamp)` is false. `timeWeight[nomineeHash]` is not updated. The function returns, leaving `timeWeight` unchanged. Subsequent calls repeat this exact path.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided, but the vulnerability relies entirely on hardcoded constants (`MAX_NUM_WEEKS = 53` and `WEEK = 604_800`).

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is highly realistic. A nominee that falls out of favor and is not checkpointed for 54 weeks will become permanently bricked. If the community later decides to revive the nominee and votes for it, their veOLAS voting power will be wasted because the nominee's weight will always evaluate to 0 during incentive distribution. An attacker can also intentionally brick dummy nominees, though this only affects the dummy nominee itself. If the entire protocol is paused or abandoned for 54 weeks, the global `timeSum` bricks, permanently destroying the tokenomics system.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH. Any nominee inactive for > 1 year is permanently destroyed and can never receive staking incentives again. If the global state is inactive for > 1 year, the entire protocol's emission distribution is permanently halted. Recovery requires a contract upgrade or complex owner intervention (e.g., removing the bricked nominee, which corrupts the global slope due to a separate bug).
recommended_mitigations: Update the state variables unconditionally at the end of the loop if the loop exhausts its iterations, allowing pagination across multiple transactions.
Change the update condition to: `if (t > block.timestamp || i == MAX_NUM_WEEKS - 1) { timeWeight[nomineeHash] = t; }`
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy `VoteWeighting`. 2. Call `addNomineeEVM`. 3. Fast forward `block.timestamp` by 54 weeks. 4. Call `checkpointNominee`. 5. Observe that `timeWeight[nomineeHash]` does not change. 6. Call `checkpointNominee` again and observe it is still stuck.
